disease.name: Hemorrhoids
disease.note: Vascular cushions in the anal canal that become symptomatic when they bleed, prolapse, or thrombose. Internal hemorrhoids (above dentate line) cause painless bleeding and prolapse. External hemorrhoids (below dentate line) cause pain when thrombosed. In Kenya, prolonged squatting for defecation and straining from constipation are common precipitants. Always examine patients presenting with rectal bleeding — never assume it is haemorrhoids without ruling out cancer.
pathophysiologyDetail: Hemorrhoids are normal anatomic vascular cushions located in the submucosa of the anal canal at the 3, 7, and 11 o'clock positions (left lateral, right anterior, right posterior) — they contribute to 15-20% of resting anal canal pressure and assist with fecal continence

Each hemorrhoidal cushion consists of a rich vascular plexus (arteriovenous anastomoses between the terminal branches of the superior rectal artery and the superior, middle, and inferior rectal veins), smooth muscle fibers (Treitz's muscle), and elastic connective tissue that anchors the cushion to the anal wall

Degeneration and fragmentation of Treitz's suspensory ligament and the surrounding connective tissue framework, caused by chronic straining and aging, allows the vascular cushions to slide distally and prolapse through the anal canal

Chronic constipation and prolonged straining during defecation generate excessive intra-abdominal pressure (up to 200 mmHg during Valsalva), which is transmitted directly to the hemorrhoidal plexus, causing venous engorgement and dilation
Prolonged squatting on pit latrines (common in rural Kenya) increases perineal descent and stretches the supporting connective tissue, accelerating hemorrhoidal prolapse and venous congestion

The internal hemorrhoidal plexus (superior rectal vein tributaries) lies above the dentate line and is covered by anorectal mucosa — bleeding from this plexus is painless because it lacks somatic innervation

The external hemorrhoidal plexus (inferior rectal vein tributaries) lies below the dentate line and is covered by anoderm (squamous epithelium with somatic sensory fibers) — thrombosis here causes excruciating pain

Arteriovenous shunting within the hemorrhoidal cushions allows direct transmission of arterial pressure from the superior rectal artery (terminal branch of the inferior mesenteric artery) to the venous plexus, explaining the bright red arterial nature of hemorrhoidal bleeding

Pregnancy induces hemorrhoidal enlargement through a combination of: (1) progesterone-mediated venous relaxation, (2) compression of the inferior vena cava and superior rectal vein by the gravid uterus, (3) increased pelvic blood flow, and (4) constipation from reduced colonic motility

Portal hypertension does NOT directly cause hemorrhoids — despite common belief, the hemorrhoidal plexus has extensive portosystemic anastomoses that decompress rather than engorge; hemorrhoids are no more prevalent in cirrhotic patients

Goligher's classification grades internal hemorrhoids by the degree of prolapse, which correlates directly with the extent of supporting tissue disruption and determines the appropriate treatment modality
Grade I hemorrhoids represent mild cushion enlargement without displacement — bleeding is the predominant symptom as the overlying mucosa becomes friable from chronic congestion
Grade II hemorrhoids prolapse through the anal canal during defecation but spontaneously reduce when the patient stands — the suspensory ligament is partially disrupted but retains some elasticity
Grade III hemorrhoids require manual reduction after each bowel movement — the supporting tissue is extensively fragmented and the cushions remain in the anal canal between defecations

Grade IV hemorrhoids are chronically prolapsed and irreducible — the vascular cushions are completely detached from their supporting structures and the overlying mucosa may become ulcerated, thrombosed, or strangulated

Thrombosis of external hemorrhoids results from acute venous stasis and rupture of a submucosal vein, forming an intravascular clot within the external hemorrhoidal plexus — the resulting distention of the anoderm's rich somatic nerve supply produces intense pain

Strangulation occurs when a prolapsed internal hemorrhoid becomes entrapped by the anal sphincter's muscular contraction, compromising venous return and arterial inflow, leading to edema, ischemia, and eventual gangrene if not reduced or excised

Spontaneous resolution of thrombosed external hemorrhoids involves clot liquefaction and organization over 7-14 days, often leaving a residual skin tag of stretched anoderm that may cause perianal hygiene issues

landmarkTrials.trial: Rubber Band Ligation vs Hemorrhoidectomy (Mullen et al.)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Rubber band ligation (RBL) vs Milligan-Morgan hemorrhoidectomy
landmarkTrials.outcome: RBL: 65% symptom-free at 1 year, 45% at 5 years. Hemorrhoidectomy: 95% symptom-free at 1 year, 85% at 5 years. RBL had less pain (p<0.001) but higher recurrence (p=0.001).
landmarkTrials.impact: Established RBL as first-line for Grade II-III with the understanding that recurrence is higher but patient acceptance is better. Hemorrhoidectomy remains definitive for advanced disease.

landmarkTrials.population: 777 patients with Grade II-IV hemorrhoids across 32 UK centers
landmarkTrials.outcome: Pain scores lower for PPH at 2 weeks (p<0.001). Return to work faster: 14 vs 21 days. Recurrence at 1 year: PPH 32% vs hemorrhoidectomy 18% (p<0.001). Prolapse recurrence higher after PPH.
landmarkTrials.impact: Landmark trial confirming PPH has less short-term pain but significantly higher long-term recurrence. Hemorrhoidectomy remains the gold standard for advanced hemorrhoids. PPH reserved for select circumferential prolapse cases.

landmarkTrials.intervention: Transanal Hemorrhoidal Dearterialization (THD) with Doppler vs Ferguson hemorrhoidectomy
landmarkTrials.outcome: THD: less postoperative pain (VAS 3.2 vs 6.1), earlier return to work (10 vs 22 days), but higher recurrence at 2 years (22% vs 8%). Bleeding recurrence similar.
landmarkTrials.impact: THD is a valid sphincter-sparing alternative with faster recovery but higher recurrence. Ideal for patients who prioritize quick return to work (common in Kenyan labor force).

landmarkTrials.trial: Excision vs Conservative for Thrombosed Hemorrhoids
landmarkTrials.population: 98 patients with acute thrombosed external hemorrhoids
landmarkTrials.intervention: Excision under LA within 72h vs conservative management (sitz baths, analgesia)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Excision: faster pain relief (24h vs 7 days), lower recurrence at 1 year (8% vs 34%), faster return to normal activity (4 vs 9 days). Conservative group had more residual skin tags.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established early excision as superior to conservative management for thrombosed external hemorrhoids — particularly for patients presenting within 72 hours of onset.

landmarkTrials.trial: High-Fiber Diet for Hemorrhoid Prevention (Moesgaard et al.)
landmarkTrials.intervention: High-fiber diet (30g/day + psyllium) vs normal diet
landmarkTrials.outcome: 6-month recurrence: 15% in fiber group vs 38% in control group (p=0.003). Reduced bleeding and prolapse in fiber group.
landmarkTrials.impact: Core evidence for dietary fiber as the foundation of hemorrhoid management. Fiber supplementation reduces recurrence after any treatment modality.

landmarkTrials.trial: Nery et al. — Sclerotherapy vs RBL for Grade II
landmarkTrials.intervention: Sclerotherapy (5% phenol in oil) vs rubber band ligation
landmarkTrials.outcome: RBL: 80% symptom control at 1 year vs sclerotherapy 68% (p=0.04). Pain: similar. Complications: RBL more secondary bleeding (3% vs 0%), sclerotherapy more injection site ulceration (5% vs 0%).
landmarkTrials.impact: RBL remains superior to sclerotherapy for Grade II hemorrhoids. Sclerotherapy reserved for patients on anticoagulation where RBL is contraindicated due to bleeding risk.

evidenceTable.indication: Gold standard for diagnosing and grading internal hemorrhoids. Essential before any treatment. Should be performed in all patients presenting with rectal bleeding.
Essential to exclude other pathology (rectal cancer, prostatic disease, fecal impaction). Cannot visualize hemorrhoids but can detect masses and assess sphincter tone.
Mandatory for all patients with alarm symptoms (weight loss, change in bowel habit, age >50, family history of CRC, dark blood mixed with stool). Excludes proximal pathology.
Adequate for young patients (<50) with typical hemorrhoidal bleeding, no alarm symptoms, and normal examination. Can visualize the left colon and rectum.
Screening tool. Positive test requires colonoscopy regardless of whether hemorrhoids are visible. Hemorrhoids can cause false positives.
Assess for iron-deficiency anemia from chronic blood loss. Low MCV and hemoglobin suggest chronic hemorrhoidal bleeding but can also indicate proximal GI pathology.
Identifies external hemorrhoids, skin tags, thrombosed hemorrhoids, and other perianal pathology (fissures, fistulas, condylomas). Essential first step in examination.
Only indicated when concomitant rectal prolapse or pelvic floor disorders suspected. Not routinely needed for hemorrhoid evaluation.

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: History — Establish nature of bleeding (bright red, on paper/in bowl/dripping), presence and reducibility of prolapse, pain (constant or only with BM), and alarm features (weight loss, change in bowel habit, age >50, family history of CRC).

Step 2: Visual Inspection — Inspect the perianal area for: external hemorrhoids, skin tags, thrombosed lumps, fissures, fistulas, condylomas, or malignant ulcers. Ask the patient to strain as if passing stool to demonstrate any prolapse.

Step 3: Digital Rectal Examination — Assess sphincter tone, identify any rectal masses, assess the prostate in men, and look for blood on the examining finger. A normal DRE does NOT exclude hemorrhoids.

Step 4: Proctoscopy — Passing a proctoscope allows direct visualization of the anal canal and identification of internal hemorrhoids. Grade them: I (non-prolapsing), II (prolapse on straining, reduce spontaneously), III (need manual reduction), IV (irreducible). Document the positions (clock face).

Step 5: Assess for Alarm Features — Any patient with: age >50, change in bowel habit, weight loss, dark blood mixed with stool, family history of CRC, or iron-deficiency anemia requires colonoscopy regardless of proctoscopy findings.

Step 6: Determine Goligher Grade — Grade I-II: office-based procedures are appropriate. Grade III-IV: surgical hemorrhoidectomy is indicated. Grade II with failed prior banding: also proceed to surgery.

Step 7: Identify Acute Complications — Thrombosed external hemorrhoid: tender blue-purple lump at verge, <72h → excision; >72h → conservative. Strangulated hemorrhoid: irreducible, edematous, cyanotic, painful → emergency hemorrhoidectomy.

Step 8: Colonoscopy — Perform colonoscopy in any patient meeting criteria above. In Kenya, where colorectal cancer often presents at a younger age, maintain a low threshold for colonoscopy even in patients <50 with 'typical' hemorrhoidal bleeding.

Step 9: Classification-Based Treatment Selection — Grade I: lifestyle + RBL. Grade II: RBL (1-3 sessions). Grade III: hemorrhoidectomy or THD. Grade IV: hemorrhoidectomy. Circumferential prolapse: consider PPH. Thrombosed <72h: excision.

Step 10: Post-Treatment Surveillance — After treatment, ensure dietary fiber and fluid intake are adequate to prevent recurrence. Schedule colonoscopy if indicated. Repeat banding in 4 weeks if needed. Wound check at 2 weeks for surgical patients.

managementPearls.pearl: Never assume rectal bleeding is from hemorrhoids without performing a proper examination (DRE + proctoscopy). In Kenya, patients with rectal bleeding are often treated symptomatically without examination, leading to missed colorectal cancer diagnoses. Any blood from the rectum demands visualization of the anal canal.
managementPearls.evidence: ASCRS Guidelines 2022; WHO CRC screening recommendations

managementPearls.pearl: Rubber band ligation is the most cost-effective office procedure for Grade I-III hemorrhoids. Apply the band 1-2 cm above the dentate line to avoid severe pain (somatic innervation below dentate line). Maximum of 3 bands per session, 4 weeks apart. Warn patients about mild ache and feeling of fullness for 24-48 hours.

For thrombosed external hemorrhoids presenting within 72 hours, office excision under local anaesthesia provides immediate relief and lower recurrence compared to conservative management. Excision of the entire clot and overlying skin ellipse is more effective than simple incision and clot evacuation.
managementPearls.evidence: Greenspon et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2005

managementPearls.pearl: Hemorrhoidectomy (Milligan-Morgan or Ferguson) remains the gold standard for Grade III-IV hemorrhoids with the lowest long-term recurrence (5-10% at 5 years). The Ferguson closed technique offers faster healing but has similar long-term outcomes to the Milligan-Morgan open technique.

Preserve at least 1 cm skin bridges between excised hemorrhoidal pedicles during hemorrhoidectomy. Resecting too much skin leads to anal stenosis — a difficult-to-manage complication requiring long-term dilatation or flap reconstruction. The 3 pedicles (left lateral, right anterior, right posterior) are separated by natural skin bridges.
managementPearls.evidence: Milligan et al., Lancet 1937; Goligher's textbook

managementPearls.pearl: Postoperative pain after hemorrhoidectomy can be significantly reduced with a multimodal regimen: oral Paracetamol, NSAIDs (if no contraindication), topical 2% diltiazem or 0.2% GTN ointment to reduce sphincter spasm, warm sitz baths, and stool softeners. Avoid opioid constipation.
managementPearls.evidence: Sammour et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2010

managementPearls.pearl: In Kenya, many patients with hemorrhoids also have concurrent anorectal conditions — anal fissure (from passing hard stools), fistula-in-ano (from previous abscess), or perianal abscess. Never focus only on the hemorrhoids; a complete anorectal examination is essential.

Pregnant women with hemorrhoids should be managed conservatively — high fiber, fluids, stool softeners, topical treatments. RBL and surgery are contraindicated during pregnancy (risk of preterm labor, infection). Most pregnancy-related hemorrhoids resolve spontaneously after delivery. Excision of thrombosed hemorrhoids can be done under LA if necessary.

Stapled hemorrhoidopexy (PPH) has fallen out of favor due to rare but serious complications: rectovaginal fistula, rectal perforation, pelvic sepsis, and chronic pain from staple line. Reserve for select cases of circumferential Grade III prolapse in patients who understand the risks. Open hemorrhoidectomy is safer and more reproducible.
managementPearls.evidence: eTHoS Trial (2016); Porrett et al., Colorectal Dis 2013

managementPearls.pearl: Boda-boda drivers and long-distance truck drivers in Kenya are at particularly high risk for hemorrhoids due to prolonged sitting and vibration. Advise them to take 5-minute breaks every 2 hours, use a cushion with a central cut-out (doughnut cushion), and maintain a high-fiber diet to prevent constipation.
managementPearls.evidence: Loder et al., Dis Colon Rectum 1994; local occupational health data

managementPearls.pearl: High-fiber diet is the foundation of hemorrhoid management and prevention. Target 25-35g fiber daily. In Kenya, affordable high-fiber options include: githeri (maize and beans), whole maize meal, sukuma wiki, brown bread, oranges, mangoes, and bananas. Advise patients to gradually increase fiber and drink adequate water to prevent bloating.
managementPearls.evidence: Moesgaard et al., Dis Colon Rectum 1982; WHO dietary guidelines

operativeNuances.nuance: In Milligan-Morgan hemorrhoidectomy, the key step is staying in the correct intersphincteric plane. Dissect the hemorrhoidal pedicle off the internal sphincter muscle fibers — the white, glistening fibers of the internal sphincter should be clearly visible at the base of the dissection. Leaving internal sphincter fibers attached to the specimen risks postoperative pain and incontinence.
operativeNuances.evidence: Milligan et al., Proc R Soc Med 1949

operativeNuances.nuance: When performing closed (Ferguson) hemorrhoidectomy, close the mucosal defect with a running 3-0 absorbable suture from the apex to the anal verge. Do not close the skin component completely — leave a small gap for drainage. Closing the skin completely risks perianal hematoma and wound infection.
operativeNuances.evidence: Ferguson et al., Surg Gynecol Obstet 1971

operativeNuances.nuance: For thrombosed external hemorrhoid excision: infiltrate local anaesthetic (1% Lidocaine with Adrenaline) in a diamond shape around the thrombosed lump. Make an ellipse incision over the apex, then dissect the entire thrombosed complex including the overlying skin and the clot. Removal of the entire specimen (not just clot evacuation) reduces recurrence from 34% to 8% at 1 year.
operativeNuances.evidence: Greenspon et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2005

operativeNuances.nuance: In rubber band ligation, position the band with a ligator (suction or forceps type). The suction ligator is preferred — it allows more precise placement. Apply the band 1-2 cm above the dentate line. If the patient experiences severe pain during application, the band is too low — remove and reposition above the dentate line.
operativeNuances.evidence: Pfenninger et al., Am Fam Physician 2002

operativeNuances.nuance: During stapled hemorrhoidopexy (PPH), the most critical step is placing the purse-string suture exactly 3-4 cm above the dentate line, incorporating only mucosa and submucosa. Too deep → includes internal sphincter or vagina (rectovaginal fistula). Too low → severe pain from somatic nerve involvement. Too high → inadequate prolapse correction.
operativeNuances.evidence: Longo et al., Osp Ital Chir 1998

operativeNuances.nuance: For strangulated hemorrhoids: emergency hemorrhoidectomy is safe and effective. Despite historical concerns about anal stenosis from excising the edematous tissue, multiple studies show similar or better outcomes compared to delayed hemorrhoidectomy. The key is preserving adequate skin bridges. Gauze packing is not needed and increases postoperative pain.
operativeNuances.evidence: Eu et al., Dis Colon Rectum 1998; Ceulemans et al., Acta Chir Belg 2001

operativeNuances.nuance: The LigaSure hemorrhoidectomy technique uses bipolar vessel sealing to dissect the hemorrhoidal pedicle with minimal thermal spread. Benefits: reduced operative time (15-20 min vs 25-35 min), less blood loss, and potentially less postoperative pain compared to conventional diathermy. However, it requires the LigaSure device which is expensive and may not be available in Kenyan district hospitals.

prognosis.naturalHistory: Hemorrhoids are a chronic, progressive condition for many patients. Without lifestyle modification, Grade I-II disease often progresses to Grade III-IV over 5-10 years. However, symptoms fluctuate — periods of exacerbation (with constipation or pregnancy) are followed by relative quiescence. Thrombosed external hemorrhoids resolve spontaneously over 7-14 days but leave residual skin tags.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: After dietary modification alone: 50-70% recurrence at 1 year. After RBL: 20-30% at 1 year, 45-55% at 5 years. After hemorrhoidectomy: 5-10% at 1 year, 15-20% at 5 years. After PPH: 18-32% at 1 year. After THD: 15-25% at 2 years. Recurrence is highest in patients who do not modify dietary habits and continue to strain.
prognosis.cancerRisk: Hemorrhoids themselves do not cause or increase the risk of colorectal cancer. However, the symptom overlap (rectal bleeding) means that colorectal cancer is initially misdiagnosed as hemorrhoids in 5-10% of patients. Any patient with rectal bleeding should have the anal canal visualized — malignancy cannot be excluded based on a 'typical' hemorrhoidal bleeding history alone.
prognosis.qualityOfLife: Hemorrhoids significantly impair quality of life through chronic bleeding, discomfort, prolapse, pruritus, and the need for perianal hygiene. Many patients avoid seeking care due to embarrassment. The social stigma of visible bleeding or a prolapsing lump can cause significant psychological distress, especially in Kenyan communities. Effective treatment dramatically improves quality of life scores.
prognosis.mortality: Hemorrhoid-associated mortality is negligible. Mortality from hemorrhoidectomy is extremely rare (<0.01%), related to anesthetic complications, massive postoperative hemorrhage, or pelvic sepsis (especially after PPH). The main mortality risk is from missed colorectal cancer misattributed to hemorrhoids — hence the critical importance of proper investigation.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Factors associated with progression and recurrence: persistent constipation and straining, low-fiber diet, inadequate fluid intake, obesity, pregnancy and childbirth, prolonged sitting (occupational), heavy lifting, and chronic cough (COPD). Protective factors: high-fiber diet (25-35g/day), adequate fluid intake (2-3L/day), regular physical activity, maintaining healthy weight, avoiding prolonged straining on the toilet, and correcting constipation.

presentingComplaints: Bright red bleeding from the back passage — blood drips into the toilet bowl or smears the toilet paper
A lump protruding from the anus during bowel movements
Pain and swelling at the anal verge (especially with thrombosed external hemorrhoid)
Itching and irritation around the anal opening
Mucous discharge and feeling of incomplete evacuation

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Strangulated/incarcerated/gangrenous/hemodynamically significant bleeding -> SEVERE; grades I-IV without complications -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Do you see blood when you pass stool? Is it on the paper, in the bowl, or dripping?
Is the blood bright red or dark?
Do you feel a lump coming out of your anus when you pass stool?
Does the lump go back in on its own or do you push it back?
Do you have pain near the anus? Is it constant or only with bowel movements?
Do you strain a lot during bowel movements?
Are you constipated often?
What is your typical diet — how much fiber do you eat?
How much water do you drink daily?
Have you noticed any weight loss recently?
Has there been any change in the caliber of your stool?
Do you have a family history of colon cancer or hemorrhoids?
Are you pregnant or have you recently given birth?
Do you sit for long periods at work?
Do you do heavy lifting as part of your job (farming, construction, boda-boda)?
Have you ever had treatment for hemorrhoids before — creams, banding, or surgery?
Have you ever had a colonoscopy?
Do you feel itchy or have a discharge around the anus?
Have you tried any creams or traditional remedies for this?
Do you feel that your bowel doesn't empty completely?
Do you use a pit latrine or a flush toilet? How long do you usually sit during defecation?
What remedies have you tried — any traditional herbs, creams, or self-treatment?
Are you a boda-boda rider, long-distance driver, or do you sit for long hours at work?
Have you ever had a blood transfusion because of bleeding from hemorrhoids?
Do you have any bleeding disorders or take blood thinners (aspirin, warfarin)?

examination_findings.finding: Visible external hemorrhoids — skin-covered lumps at the anal verge
Internal hemorrhoids seen on proctoscopy — cherry-red swellings above the dentate line
Thrombosed external hemorrhoid — tense, blue-purple, tender lump at anal verge
Prolapsing hemorrhoids that appear on straining — can be reducible or incarcerated
Digital rectal examination — normal sphincter tone, no rectal mass (must exclude cancer)
Perianal skin tags from previous thrombosed hemorrhoids
Mucous discharge on the perianal skin
Anal fissure may coexist — assess carefully
Pallor suggesting chronic blood loss anemia
Grade internal hemorrhoids: I (non-prolapsing), II (prolapse and reduce spontaneously), III (need manual reduction), IV (irreducible)
Strangulated hemorrhoid — irreducible, edematous, ischemic, and painful
Circumferential prolapse — all 3 primary pedicles prolapsed simultaneously, suggestive of Grade IV disease requiring PPH or open hemorrhoidectomy
Skin tags at previous thrombosed hemorrhoid sites — may cause difficulty with hygiene
Anal canal stenosis on DRE — from prior hemorrhoidectomy with excessive mucosal excision
Concurrent anal fissure on inspection — common comorbidity from passing hard stools

bedside_scores.name: Goligher Classification of Internal Hemorrhoids

components.name: Grade I — Hemorrhoids bleed but do not prolapse
Grade II — Prolapse on straining but reduce spontaneously
Grade III — Prolapse requiring manual reduction
Grade IV — Irreducible prolapse (may be incarcerated)

bedside_scores.interpretation: Grade I-II: conservative or office-based procedures. Grade III-IV: surgical hemorrhoidectomy.

bedside_scores.name: Hemorrhoid Bleeding Severity Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: 1-2: conservative/office treatment. 3-5: intervention needed. 6-8: urgent intervention, consider transfusion.

riskFactors.note: Hormonal changes and increased venous pressure
Occupational hazard for drivers, office workers

subtypes.name: Internal Hemorrhoids (Goligher Classification)
subtypes.management: Grade I-II: Lifestyle modification (high fiber, increased fluids, stool softeners). Rubber band ligation (RBL) — gold standard office procedure. Sclerotherapy. Infrared coagulation. Grade III-IV: Hemorrhoidectomy (Milligan-Morgan open or Ferguson closed). Stapled hemorrhoidopexy (PPH) for circumferential prolapse. THD (Transanal Hemorrhoidal Dearterialization).

subtypes.name: External Hemorrhoids
subtypes.management: Asymptomatic: no treatment needed. Thrombosed <72h: excision under local anaesthesia — immediate relief. Thrombosed >72h: conservative — warm baths, topical anesthetics, oral analgesia (Paracetamol). Large symptomatic: hemorrhoidectomy.

subtypes.name: Thrombosed External Hemorrhoid
subtypes.management: Excision under local anaesthesia if within 72 hours of onset — immediate pain relief, lower recurrence. Conservative if >72 hours: warm sitz baths, topical lidocaine, stool softeners, oral analgesics. Resolution over 7-14 days.

subtypes.name: Strangulated Hemorrhoids
subtypes.management: Emergency hemorrhoidectomy — excise all gangrenous tissue, preserve skin bridges. This is safe and effective despite historical concerns. IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics if gangrenous changes. Postoperative stool softeners, sitz baths, analgesia. Delayed treatment increases risk of anal stenosis and sphincter damage.

subtypes.name: Mixed Internal-External Hemorrhoids
subtypes.management: Milligan-Morgan or Ferguson hemorrhoidectomy — excise both internal and external components in continuity. The external component must be excised to ensure complete resolution; leaving external tags behind leads to patient dissatisfaction. Preservation of skin bridges is essential to prevent stenosis.

bedside.name: Digital rectal examination
bedside.indication: rule out other pathology (mass, fissure, prostate), assess sphincter tone

bedside.name: Proctoscopy (in clinic or OR)
bedside.indication: visualize internal hemorrhoids, grade them, assess for synchronous pathology

laboratory.name: Complete Blood Count
laboratory.indication: if chronic bleeding to assess for anemia

imaging.name: Colonoscopy
imaging.indication: mandatory if age >50, alarm symptoms (weight loss, change in bowel habit), or family history of CRC

imaging.name: Flexible sigmoidoscopy
imaging.indication: if younger patient <50 with typical hemorrhoidal bleeding

interpretation_rules.always_examine: Never attribute rectal bleeding to hemorrhoids without performing a digital rectal exam and proctoscopy. Age >50 or alarm symptoms require full colonoscopy.
interpretation_rules.hemorrhoid_grade_treatment: Grade I-II respond to office procedures (RBL). Grade III-IV require surgery. Grade II with failure of RBL also requires surgery.
interpretation_rules.thrombosed_hemorrhoid_timing: Excision should be performed only if within 72h of onset. After 72h, the clot organizes and excision is no more effective than conservative management.
interpretation_rules.concurrent_pathology: Hemorrhoids commonly coexist with anal fissures, fistulas, and skin tags. Always examine the entire perianal area.

management.initial: Dietary modification: high fiber (25-35g/day), increased oral fluids (2-3L/day), avoid straining. Stool softeners (Psyllium/Metamucil or Lactulose). Warm sitz baths. Topical treatments (lidocaine, hydrocortisone suppositories/cream for short term). Avoid prolonged sitting on the toilet.
management.definitive: Grade I-II: Rubber band ligation (RBL) — office-based, 1-3 sessions, 4 weeks apart. Sclerotherapy (5% phenol in oil) for bleeding. Infrared coagulation. Grade III-IV: Hemorrhoidectomy — Milligan-Morgan (open, excise 3 pedicles) or Ferguson (closed, sutured) — gold standard. Stapled hemorrhoidopexy (PPH) for circumferential prolapse — less pain but higher recurrence. THD (Doppler-guided hemorrhoidal artery ligation). Thrombosed <72h: excision under LA. Grade IV strangulated: emergency hemorrhoidectomy.
management.postop: Sitz baths 2-3 times daily and after BM. Stool softeners (Lactulose) for 2-4 weeks. Analgesia (Paracetamol ± NSAIDs). Avoid straining. Wound care — healing over 2-6 weeks. Return to work 1-4 weeks depending on procedure.

pathways.criteria: bleeding or prolapse that reduces spontaneously, no pain
pathways.treatment: Dietary modification (high fiber, increased fluid). Stool softeners. Rubber band ligation (1-3 sessions). Sclerotherapy or infrared coagulation as alternatives.

pathways.criteria: prolapse requiring manual reduction or irreducible, with or without bleeding
pathways.treatment: Hemorrhoidectomy (Milligan-Morgan or Ferguson). Stapled hemorrhoidopexy for circumferential prolapse. THD as alternative. Postoperative stool softeners and analgesia.

pathways.criteria: acute painful lump at anal verge, <72h duration for excision
pathways.treatment: Excision under local anaesthesia if <72h. Conservative management if >72h: warm baths, topical anesthetics, oral analgesia, stool softeners.

pathways.criteria: irreducible prolapse with ischemic changes, severe pain
pathways.treatment: Emergency hemorrhoidectomy. IV fluids. Analgesia. Broad-spectrum antibiotics if gangrenous changes. Postop wound care and stool softeners.

operative_templates.indication: Grade III-IV internal hemorrhoids, or mixed internal-external hemorrhoids
operative_templates.incision: Elliptical incisions over each hemorrhoidal pedicle (usually 3: left lateral, right anterior, right posterior)
steps: Position patient in lithotomy, prep and drape
Gentle anal dilatation (Hill-Ferguson or Sims retractor)
Identify the 3 primary hemorrhoidal pedicles (left lateral, right anterior, right posterior)
Grasp the hemorrhoidal pedicle with forceps (Allis)
Make an elliptical incision around the pedicle, from perianal skin to the anal canal
Dissect the pedicle from the underlying internal sphincter muscle
Ligate the pedicle at its apex with absorbable suture (transfixion ligation)
Excise the hemorrhoid distal to the ligature
Leave the wound open to heal by secondary intention
Repeat for remaining pedicles
Ensure at least 1cm skin bridges are preserved between excisions to prevent anal stenosis
Apply haemostatic dressing

operative_templates.indication: Grade III-IV internal hemorrhoids, especially when rapid healing desired
steps: Position and prepare as for Milligan-Morgan
Identify and dissect hemorrhoidal pedicles
Excise the hemorrhoid tissue from the pedicle
Carefully close the mucosal defect with running absorbable suture (3-0 Vicryl or chromic)
Close the skin component of the wound loosely
Preserve skin bridges between excisions
Repeat for remaining pedicles
Apply dressing

operative_templates.indication: Acute thrombosed external hemorrhoid within 72 hours of onset
operative_templates.incision: Elliptical incision over the thrombosed lump
steps: Position patient in lithotomy
Clean the perianal area with antiseptic
Infiltrate 5-10ml of 1% Lidocaine with Adrenaline around and under the thrombosed hemorrhoid
Make an elliptical incision over the thrombosed lump
Dissect the entire thrombosed hemorrhoid including the clot and the overlying skin
Remove the specimen intact — this dramatically reduces recurrence
Close the defect loosely with a single absorbable suture (optional)
Apply pressure dressing

dischargeCriteria: Patient educated on sitz baths and wound care
Colonoscopy arranged if indicated (age >50 or alarm symptoms)

patient_education.explanation: Haemorrhoids are swollen blood vessels in the anal canal — think of them as varicose veins of the back passage. Almost everyone has hemorrhoidal tissue, but it becomes a problem when it bleeds, prolapses (comes out), or becomes thrombosed (clots). In Kenya, prolonged squatting during defecation and constipation from low-fiber diets are common causes. Treatment ranges from simple diet and lifestyle changes to office-bandings to surgery — depending on the severity.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital if you have: heavy bleeding (soaking through a pad), severe pain not controlled by medication, fever with chills, inability to pass urine after surgery, or if a prolapsed hemorrhoid becomes dark/black suggesting gangrene.
patient_education.activity: Avoid heavy lifting and straining for 2 weeks after banding and 4 weeks after hemorrhoidectomy. Walking is encouraged from day 1. Avoid prolonged sitting — take breaks every 30 minutes. If your job involves driving, return after 1-2 weeks for office procedures, 3-4 weeks for surgery.
patient_education.diet: The most important treatment is to prevent constipation. Eat high-fiber foods: fruits (mangoes, pawpaw, oranges, bananas), vegetables (sukuma wiki, cabbage, spinach), whole grains (brown bread, oats, unprocessed maize). Drink at least 8-10 glasses of water daily. Take Psyllium (Metamucil) or Lactulose daily if prescribed. Avoid excessive chapati, white rice, and fried foods.
patient_education.medication: Use pain medication as prescribed. Take warm sitz baths for 10-15 minutes after each bowel movement and 2-3 times daily. Apply any prescribed creams sparingly. If you had rubber band ligation, you may have mild discomfort and a sensation of fullness for 24-48 hours. If you had hemorrhoidectomy, the wounds will heal over 2-6 weeks.
patient_education.followUp: After banding, return for repeat sessions in 4 weeks if needed. After hemorrhoidectomy, return in 2 weeks for wound check. If you are over 50 or have any concerning symptoms, you need a colonoscopy to ensure there is no other cause for the bleeding.

followup.before_discharge: Ensure pain controlled. Confirm patient has passed urine. Educate on sitz baths. Prescribe stool softeners. Arrange colonoscopy if indicated.

after_discharge.1_week: Wound check (if surgery). Assess for urinary retention, bleeding, pain control. Ensure stool softeners working.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Assess wound healing. Review pathology if tissue sent. Schedule further banding sessions if needed. Discuss dietary compliance.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Confirm complete healing. Assess for anal stenosis. Discuss long-term fiber and lifestyle modification. Discharge if recovered.
after_discharge.3_months: Only if ongoing issues. Ensure colonoscopy has been performed if indicated.

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: heavy bleeding (not just spotting), severe pain unrelieved by medication, inability to pass urine, fever, wound discharge, or a prolapsed lump that becomes dark/black.
